text.title: Freelance MSA + SOW Templates Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules define the contractual primitives a P3 freelancer signs every month — split between long-lived MSA and per-engagement SOW, with explicit handling of money, IP, warranty and exit.

rule.statement: The contract package is split into exactly two documents: an MSA that defines everlasting terms (IP regime, confidentiality, indemnity caps, governing law, dispute resolution, payment cycle, termination triggers) and a SOW per engagement that defines deliverables, milestones, price, and acceptance. SOWs may amend the MSA's specifics for one engagement only; they may not silently override IP or indemnity.

rationale.source: MSA + SOW pattern needed for monthly contract signing

rule.rationale: A single mega-contract per engagement forces re-negotiation of stable terms each time; an MSA-then-SOW split lets the freelancer re-sign in days because only deliverables and price are debated.

rule.statement: Payment terms in every SOW must include: (a) an upfront deposit of at least 25% of the SOW value invoiced before work begins, (b) weekly or biweekly progress invoices for T&M work or milestone invoices for fixed-price, (c) net-14 payment terms with a defined late-fee clause (e.g. 1.5%/month or statutory rate, whichever is higher). "Pay on delivery" or "net-60" terms without a corresponding price uplift are explicitly rejected.

rationale.source: Deposit + weekly invoicing — solo cashflow primitive

rule.rationale: Solo freelancers cannot finance a buyer's 60-day payment terms; deposits and weekly invoicing keep cashflow positive and reveal payment problems early enough to stop work without taking a fatal loss.

rule.statement: Intellectual property transfers to the client on full payment of the relevant invoice, not on signature or on delivery. The MSA names the regime as work-for-hire-or-assignment-on-payment with a fallback assignment clause, explicitly carves out: (a) the freelancer's pre-existing IP (libraries, tools, templates) which is licensed back perpetually-royalty-free, (b) general know-how which is not transferred.

rationale.source: IP transfer on payment is the freelancer's leverage

rule.rationale: Transfer-on-signature gives away the only leverage if the client refuses to pay; transfer-on-payment keeps the work owned by the freelancer until cash clears, which is enforceable in most jurisdictions and is the freelance default for a reason.

rule.statement: Warranty is bounded in two dimensions: scope (delivered code matches the SOW's acceptance criteria; third-party libraries and client-provided systems are excluded) and time (e.g. 30 or 60 days post-acceptance for defect fixes free of charge; beyond that any remediation is a new SOW or T&M). Implied warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, and non-infringement are limited to the contract price ceiling.

rationale.source: Warranty clause must be bounded — solo freelancer cannot underwrite unbounded liability

rule.rationale: An unbounded warranty (e.g. "shall be free of defects for ever") effectively makes the freelancer a perpetual on-call resource for free, which is fatal to a solo practice. Explicit bounds preserve the contract economics.

rule.statement: Termination clauses cover three cases: (a) termination for cause (material breach, 14-day cure period), (b) termination for convenience by either party with notice (typically 14 days) and pro-rata payment for work completed up to termination, (c) termination on non-payment after a defined cure period during which the freelancer may suspend work. The MSA explicitly states what survives termination (confidentiality, IP regime for paid work, dispute resolution, accrued payment).

rationale.source: Termination is the escape clause that prevents toxic engagements

rule.rationale: Without a termination-for-convenience clause the freelancer is trapped in deteriorating engagements; without a non-payment trigger the freelancer must continue working while invoices age. Both clauses are insurance the freelancer routinely needs to invoke.

example.title: MSA + SOW pair for a 10-week engagement
example.code:
MSA (signed once, lives for the relationship): §3 Payment cycle: net-14, late fee 1.5%/mo §4 IP: assignment on payment; pre-existing IP listed at Annex A is licensed back royalty-free; general know-how excluded §5 Warranty: 60 days post-acceptance per SOW; liability cap = SOW value; no implied merchantability beyond cap §6 Termination: for-cause with 14d cure; for-convenience with 14d notice; non-payment suspension after 14d past-due §7 Confidentiality: 3 years post-termination §8 Governing law / venue: agreed (e.g. Portugal / Lisbon arbitration)
SOW-01 (signed per engagement): Scope: described as 4 milestones M1-M4 with acceptance criteria each Price: €36,000 fixed across 4 milestones (€9k each) Deposit: €9,000 (25% = first milestone) invoiced and paid before kickoff Cadence: milestone invoice on each acceptance, net-14 Out of scope: SSO integration, multi-tenant data partitioning Acceptance: client confirms in writing within 7d of demo or is deemed accepted
Result: SOW is 2 pages, MSA is 8 pages, total signature cycle = 5 business days.
example.note: The MSA does the heavy contractual lifting once. Every subsequent SOW with the same client is a short, fast-to-sign document about deliverables and money.
